steps.title: Identify the two independent clauses
steps.content: The sentence has two independent clauses joined at the blank: It is generally true that technological change is a linear process, in which once-useful technologies are replaced by new and better ones and the reawakening of interest in the steam engine (from advocates of carbon-neutral rail travel) reminds us that ostensibly obsolete technologies may be brought back into service to address society’s changing needs. The transition word “even so” must link them with the right punctuation.

steps.title: Test each choice for correct semicolon placement
steps.content:
A semicolon must come before a conjunctive adverb (like “even so”), and a comma follows it — that is the only standard pattern.
  • Choice A — ones, even so;: places the semicolon after “even so,” which attaches it to the wrong clause and produces an illogical sentence. ✗
  • Choice B — ones even so,: omits the semicolon entirely, creating a run-on sentence. ✗
  • Choice C — ones; even so,: correctly places the semicolon after “ones” and a comma after “even so,” joining the two independent clauses in standard fashion. ✓
  • Choice D — ones, even so,: uses only commas, creating a comma splice without a coordinating conjunction. ✗
C is correct.
